content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 4,
1993
Steven Bruckman
Deputy City Attorney
Office of City Attorney
City of Roseville
311 Vernon Street,
Room 202
Roseville,
CA
95678
E.
Garth Black
Cooper,
White and Cooper
201 California Street,
17th Floor
San Francisco,
CA
94111
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93—407
Dear Mr.
Bruckman and Mr.
Black:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the City of Roseville and Roseville Telephone Company
regarding the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act
11
) •1
Since you are requesting general
guidance with respect to the application of the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act,
we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance.
2
QUESTION
Does the “public generally” exception permit public officials
with investment interests in Roseville Telephone Company to
participate in decisions that will foreseeably and materially
affect Roseville Telephone company?
-
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93-407
Page
2
CONCLUS ION
Where an official has an interest in a business entity, the
“public generally” exception applies when a decision will affect a
significant segment of the public in substantially the same manner
as the decision would affect the business.
The “public generally”
exception does not apply to the facts you provided.
Thus, the
officials may not participate in the decisions.
FACTS
Roseville Telephone Company (the “company”)
is locally owned
and provides substantially all the telephone service in the City
of Roseville.
You stated that a large portion of the population
of the city own stock in the company.
In the past we have advised that councilmembers who own stock
in Roseville Telephone Company with a value of $1,000 or more were
disqualified from any decision that would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Roseville Telephone
Company.
(Dean Advice Letter,
No.
1-88-316; Sections 87100 and
87103.)
You have asked that we reconsider this advice in light of
the recent adoption of Regulation 18703.
Specifically,
you have asked whether a public official with
an investment interest in the company
(as defined in Section
87103)
may participate in general plan and zoning matters that
will foreseeably and materially affect Roseville Telephone
Company.
You also asked whether the official could participate in the
decision if 4,000 of the 20,000 households
(20 percent)
in the
jurisdiction included individuals that had investment interests in
Roseville Telephone Company.
DISCUSSION
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from makIng,
participating in making,
or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest.
Section 87103 provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
oron:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth


Our File No.
1—93-407
Page
3
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
***
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
(Emphasis added.)
Your question involves officials who have investments in
Roseville Telephone Company worth more than $1,000.
Thus, the
officials would be prohibited from participating in decisions that
will foreseeably
3 and materially affect Roseville Telephone
Company.
Regulation 18702.2 applies where a decision will have an
indirect financial effect on a business entity in which the
official has an interest.
Thus, if an official has an investment
interest in Roseville Telephone Company,
or has received income
from or is employed by Roseville Telephone Company, the official
would be prohibited from participating in any decision that will
affect Roseville Telephone in the following ways:
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
1—93—407
Page
4
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $150,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $150,000 or more.
Regulation 18702.2(b)
and (e).
Thus, despite the fact that the decision will not affect the
actual value of an official’s investment,
or the amount of income
an official receives from Roseville Telephone Company, the
official would be disqualified because it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the business entity.
4
“Public Generally” Exception
You have asked specifically about decisions that will have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on Roseville Telephone
Company.
Public officials may participate in decisions that
foreseeably and materially affect their interests if the effect on
the official’s interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.
On September 7,
1993, the Commission adopted specific
standards to determine when a governmental decision will fall
within the “public generally” exception.
New Regulation 18703
provides that the “public generally” exception applies where both
subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
Significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
Section 87103 also recognizes direct effects on an official and
his or her immediate family as an alternate independent basis of
disqualification.
Regulation 18702.1 provides that decisions that
do affect the official and his or her immediate family are
material where the “decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets
(other than interests in real property),
or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing by at least $250.”
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (4).)


Our File No.
1-93-407
Page
5
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade,
or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
***
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
(Emphasis added.)
Thus, the test is whether the decision will affect the public
generally,
or a significant segment of the public generally,
in
substantially the same manner as the decision will affect the
official’s economic interest.
Section 87103 provides that the analysis is applied to the
economic interest of the official, rather than the official
personally.
5
This is because the statute provides that the
business entity
(not the stock)
is the economic interest and the
official must disqualify himself if the business entity is
Section 87103 provides:
“An official has a financial interest
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on.
. .Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.”


Our File No.
1—93—407
Page
6
materially affected pursuant to Regulation 18702.2.
(Section
87103; Levinger Advice Letter,
No.
A—87—061; Blegan Advice Letter,
A—85—176; Waggoner Advice Letter,
No.
A—85-089, copies enclosed.)
Consequently, if the decision materially affects a business
entity which is an economic interest of the official, the segment
must be affected in the same manner as the business entity——not
the official.
Even if the segment of households with persons
owning stock was a significant segment,
on the basis of your facts
this segment would not be affected in the same manner as Roseville
Telephone Company and therefore the exception would not apply.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
ENCLOSURES
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
al Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10 per page.

with investment interests in Roseville Telephone Company to
participate in decisions that will foreseeably and materially
affect Roseville Telephone company?

Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)

Our File No.
1—93-407

CONCLUSION: Where an official has an interest in a business entity, the
“public generally” exception applies when a decision will affect a
significant segment of the public in substantially the same manner
as the decision would affect the business.
The “public generally”
exception does not apply to the facts you provided.
Thus, the
officials may not participate in the decisions.
